text:
Data scarcity remains a formidable obstacle in the development of deep learning (DL) models, which traditionally require vast datasets to achieve high performance. In many specialized fields, obtaining sufficient labeled data is difficult because the annotation process necessitates expert knowledge and is inherently costly, time-consuming, and prone to human error. This dependency on large-scale datasets often discourages the adoption of DL in domains where data is limited. Ensuring data sufficiency and quality is the foundational step toward building successful and reliable DL applications. 

This paper provides a holistic survey of state-of-the-art techniques designed to train DL models under constrained conditions, specifically targeting three core challenges: small datasets, class imbalance, and poor generalization. The review begins by categorizing fundamental learning techniques and DL architectures. It then provides an in-depth analysis of modern solutions for data scarcity, including Transfer Learning (TL), Self-Supervised Learning (SSL), Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), specialized Model Architectures (MA), Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINN), and Deep Synthetic Minority Oversampling Techniques (DeepSMOTE). Furthermore, the paper offers practical strategies for data acquisition and recommendations for ensuring the trustworthiness of training sets. Finally, we examine various applications currently hampered by data scarcity—ranging from Medical Imaging and Fluid Mechanics to Cybersecurity and Wireless Communications—and propose specific data-generation alternatives for each. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first review to offer such an exhaustive overview of strategies to mitigate data scarcity in deep learning.
